D5 Design L3 Apply Reference

Bauhaus: Shape Meets Colour

Itten and Kandinsky paired each primary shape with a primary colour — triangle-yellow, square-red, circle-blue

At the Bauhaus, Itten and Kandinsky argued that the three primary shapes carry the three primary colours. The acute triangle is restless and bright — yellow. The stable square is grounded and weighty — red. The endless circle is calm and deep — blue. A correspondence of feeling, taught as design law.

Select a shape to see its assigned colour and Kandinsky's rationale. Whether the pairing is universal or cultural is still debated — but as a Bauhaus teaching tool it shaped a century of design.
